# Questions in Livestream 247

Live Stream #247: A Hamburger Today For a Scarab Tomorrow

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=564" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:24</a> | How much would someone have to change a question before it counts as a different question? | A lot; he says if he can still recognize it as the same question, it is too close to count as a new one. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=665" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:05</a> | What is happening in Deuteronomy 22:28-29 with the woman involved? | He says the passage has to be read in context and distinguishes a consensual discovered act from the earlier rape case where the woman cried out and no one could save her. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=1031" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:11</a> | Was fifty shekels enough to buy a slave in that period? | Yes, more than enough; he says it was a large amount of money. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=1067" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:47</a> | What was the shekel-to-scarab conversion rate? | He jokes that a shekel would buy a lot of scarabs because scarabs were cheap. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=1192" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:52</a> | Why were the Serabit el-Khadim mines seasonal while Timna and Faynan could operate year-round? | He says the answer is mainly climate, with Serabit becoming brutally hot in summer and much harder to work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=1336" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">22:16</a> | Did the Egyptians have a 360-day year? | No; he says they had a 365-day year, but the missing quarter day made the civil calendar rotate through the seasons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=1479" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:39</a> | Did Pharaoh Merneptah have a firstborn son, and when did he die? | He says Merneptah's firstborn son, Seti-Merneptah, outlived him and ruled as Seti II. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=1597" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:37</a> | How did usurpers get military support to defect and help them seize the throne? | He says sidelined royal sons were often pushed into the military, where they could build the relationships and backing needed for a bid for power. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=1825" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">30:25</a> | If Merneptah went past the Shephelah and back down, why do maps often place Yanoam by the Sea of Galilee? | He says Yanoam's location is uncertain, and placing it by Galilee is more an average of proposals than a secure identification. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=2036" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:56</a> | Did ancient soldiers have ways to keep their swords from going blunt? | Yes; they used sharpening stones, though the need depended on whether the weapon was iron or hardened bronze. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=2271" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:51</a> | Are Old Testament concepts like sacred space, uncleanness, and ritual washing actual phenomenological realities or cultural accommodation for didactic purposes? | He takes a middle position: sacred space and uncleanness involve real divine holiness, while ritual actions are performative accommodations for relationship rather than merely didactic devices. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=2500" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:40</a> | Why did Egypt stop practicing royal human sacrifice early while Mesopotamia did not? | He credits the power of the Egyptian bureaucracy, which became strong enough to shut the practice down. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=2698" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:58</a> | Was there tension between Egypt's military and bureaucracy if controlling the military meant controlling the country? | Yes; he says the military was necessary but not sufficient, because the bureaucracy and priesthood controlled food supplies and could constrain the army. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=2863" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:43</a> | Did the nomes have their own mini-bureaucracies? | Yes, but local scribes generally worked under the central bureaucracy centered at Memphis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=2930" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:50</a> | Can he recommend a good Bible study on Revelation? | No; he says he wishes there were a good one and does not know one he can recommend. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=3376" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:16</a> | If a woman teaches biblical studies at a seminary, is she violating 1 Timothy 2:12? | He answers by stressing the passage's church-order context rather than treating every academic teaching role as identical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=3996" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:06:36</a> | What would be the proper title for Daniel's position in Babylon? | He is not sure; Daniel had authority and was known as an interpreter of dreams, but the exact administrative title is unclear to him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=4067" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:07:47</a> | How much does he trust modern facial reconstruction methods for ancient individuals? | Very little; he says reconstructions vary heavily by artist and are more artistic curiosities than reliable scientific results. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=4303" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:43</a> | What is meant by the fourth generation in Genesis 15? | He says it is not a literal four generations, but language of completion and possibly the development of clan structures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=4518" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:18</a> | Is 1 Chronicles 7:25-27 an abbreviated genealogy? | No; he says it is doing something else, involving clan survival and a later tribal-chief framework rather than a shortened genealogy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=4990" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:23:10</a> | What does he think of Brian Windle's suggestion that Israel caused only small-scale destruction at Jericho and Hazor because the biblical author used hyperbolic rhetoric? | He rejects it as wishful thinking, saying the proposed 1360 BC conquest has no matching destruction evidence at Hazor. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=5402" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:02</a> | Does the Thutmose IV scarab found in the Hazor tomb area pose a problem for the early-date conquest? | He says it is a problem for 1400 BC Masoretic-text maximalists, but the larger problem for 1360 BC views is the lack of a Hazor destruction then. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=5562" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:42</a> | On a scale of 0 to 10, how convinced is he that Sennacherib's throne room contains an image of Hezekiah and Jerusalem? | About a one; he thinks Sennacherib's Jerusalem campaign was something the Assyrians would want to gloss over rather than commemorate. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=5832" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:12</a> | If Egyptian chariots held two people and Hittite chariots held three, how many did Canaanite chariots usually carry? | He says the evidence is limited, but northern Israel sold chariots and horses to the Hittites, so some were made in Hittite style for three people. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=5977" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:39:37</a> | Why was iron sometimes worth far more than gold in the Bronze Age? | Because it was scarce and largely meteoritic before iron smelting, making it much rarer than later people assume. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=6336" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:36</a> | Were the iron chariots among the Canaanites literal or figurative in Joshua 17:16 and Judges 1:19? | Literal, but not all-iron vehicles; he says the iron was likely in practical parts such as axle assemblies and wheel treads. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=7404" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:24</a> | If an archaeological stone says A and a text says B, should one simply go with the stone? | No; he says the sounder method is to ask why the text says B in light of A, whether the text is being misread, and whether the archaeological data is being used in the right context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=7766" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:26</a> | How can someone study history without getting emotional? | He says it is hard not to react emotionally when bad claims are repeated constantly, but the answer is to keep working from evidence rather than the claims. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=8249" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:29</a> | Why do some people push for separate secular evidence of Joseph? | He says motives vary, including desperation, cherry-picking, poor method, and sometimes outright grifting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=8487" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:21:27</a> | Has he seen the debate between Inspiring Philosophy and Muslim Metaphysician, and what does he think? | No; he says he has not watched it because publishing commitments kept him from following online debates. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=8547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:27</a> | What does he think of the claim that there was no First Intermediate Period in Egypt? | He rejects it as nonsense and as chronology-shortening driven by prior commitments rather than the evidence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=8749" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:25:49</a> | Did ancient warriors use melee spears or throwing spears like javelins? | Both, but in different roles: infantry typically carried melee spears, while charioteers used javelins and could also have other weapons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=8938" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:28:58</a> | What does he think of Ortiz and Coyle's article on Merneptah and Israel in the Transjordan? | He has not read it yet, though he owns the volume and contributed an essay to it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=9086" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:31:26</a> | Could he make a Monday video tracing backwards from modern history into Egyptian history as far as dates remain reliable? | No; he says that would be dissertation-sized, though he sketches the synchronisms and says dates get fuzzy around the First Intermediate Period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=9641" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:41</a> | Do we have signed personal accounts or eyewitness citations from the first century like skeptics demand of New Testament documents? | No; he says that is an unreasonable standard because ancient works were cataloged differently and usually dictated through scribes rather than personally signed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=9923" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:45:23</a> | When does sin cut someone off from a state of grace? | He says the real dividing line is an unrepentant state of chronic sin, not a single isolated failure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=11041" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:04:01</a> | Is he Catholic or Protestant? | He does not reveal his denomination or broad denominational category, only that he and Mrs. Producer are Christians. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=11121" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:05:21</a> | Did God take away Pharaoh's free will in the Exodus? | No; he says Pharaoh first chose to harden his heart, and God then channeled or confirmed that chosen path. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=11266" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:07:46</a> | Did the saints actually resurrect in Matthew, or was that apocalyptic language? | He says both: the language is apocalyptic, but it has force only if there was some actual resurrection, probably of recently deceased saints. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=11388" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:09:48</a> | In an English-dominated modern world, should scholars still learn languages like German and French, and which language would he recommend? | Yes; he says German and French remain scholarly languages for most fields, while Chinese is needed only if the field requires it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=11509" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:49</a> | How many languages does he speak? | He says he only speaks English, but reads thirteen natural ancient and modern languages besides English. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=11604" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:24</a> | Does he know Spanish? | No; he says Mrs. Producer has taken Spanish, but he has only picked up a few words and his Spanish is weak. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=12002" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:20:02</a> | Does he agree more with Christians who advocate full abolition of abortion or with incrementalists, and is there biblical justification for one side? | He says there are biblical justifications for both approaches; he would welcome full abolition but does not take a hard strategic position. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=12245" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:24:05</a> | Does Genesis 31:53 say Nahor and Terah worshiped God alongside Abraham? | No; he says the Hebrew uses a plural verb, indicating the God of Abraham and the god of Nahor are different, and Joshua 24:2 supports that reading. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=12580" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:40</a> | Is the plural in Genesis 31:53 because Elohim is plural for both the God of Abraham and Nahor? | No; he says a majestic plural still takes a singular verb, while this passage uses a plural verb. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=13296" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:41:36</a> | Should 2 Samuel 21 be understood as human sacrifice, and did the death of Saul's descendants end the famine? | He says it is not human sacrifice but token retributive justice for Saul's breach of the Gibeonite covenant, allowing the covenant terms to resume. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=13702" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:48:22</a> | What happens to gods when they starve? | He jokes that Mesopotamian gods do not die when deprived of offerings; they waste away and complain. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=13862" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:51:02</a> | Is Genesis written as a polemic against Egyptian religion and cosmology? | To a small extent, but he says Genesis 1 is more broadly religious and ritual prologue than simply polemic. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/hz1C0VPD310?t=14076" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:36</a> | Is he aware of John R. Huddleston's critique of Kitchen and his attempt to place the plagues in an Egyptian setting? | Only vaguely; he says he does not know Huddleston's arguments well enough, but a claim that similar plagues occur elsewhere would not by itself discredit the Egyptian setting. |
